description: The provided image is an irrelevant software interface icon with the text 'Check for updates', which has no correlation with the included research content about Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel. No valid material science-related figure content, including microstructure characterization images, mechanical property curves, phase structure analysis results or processing flow diagrams matching the paper, can be identified from this image, so effective material science information extraction for the target research cannot be completed based on the current image.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to three different hot rolling reductions: HR45 (45% reduction, subfigures a,d,g), HR68 (68% reduction, subfigures b,e,h), HR82 (82% reduction, subfigures c,f,i). Subfigures (a-c) are band contrast maps; (d-f) are phase maps overlaid with grain boundary maps, where blue denotes face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite phase, black lines represent high-angle grain boundaries (misorientation ≥ 15°), red lines represent Σ3 (60°⟨111⟩) annealing twin boundaries. Subfigures (g-i) are Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps with KAM angle ranging from 0° to 5°, where regions with high orientation gradient indicate local strain concentration. As hot rolling reduction increases, austenite grain size significantly refines (average grain size without twins decreases from 45.11 μm to 18.80 μm, effective grain size including twins reduces from 21.82 μm to 7.02 μm), the fraction of Σ3 twin boundaries reduces from 67% to 52%, and KAM maps show more obvious local strain concentration corresponding to higher geometrically necessary dislocation density. The results confirm that higher hot rolling reduction induces grain refinement via enhanced dynamic recrystallization and increased dislocation accumulation, providing microstructure basis for improved mechanical properties.

This group of SEM backscattered electron (BSE) images visualize the distribution of second-phase precipitates in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different hot rolling reductions: HR45 (45% reduction, subplots a, d), HR68 (68% reduction, subplots b, e), HR82 (82% reduction, subplots c, f). Subplots a-c with a 20 μm scale bar show micron-sized polygonal (Nb,Mo,W)C particles distributed along the rolling direction, while subplots d-f with a 300 nm scale bar present submicron-sized spherical (Nb,Mo,W)C particles at higher magnification. No obvious difference in the size or volume fraction of (Nb,Mo,W)C precipitates is observed among the three samples. These (Nb,Mo,W)C particles act as particle-stimulated nucleation sites for dynamic recrystallization to refine grains, and contribute to precipitation strengthening, supporting the core conclusion that increased hot rolling reduction improves the mechanical properties of the investigated low-density steel via multiple cooperative strengthening mechanisms.

This figure characterizes a submicron-sized precipitate in the HR68 Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel sample (hot rolling reduction of 68%, water quenched after rolling). Subfigure (a) is the TEM bright-field image showing the ~256 nm spherical precipitate morphology with a 100 nm scale bar. Subfigures (b-h) are corresponding energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental maps for Fe, Mn, Al, C, Nb, Mo, W respectively, revealing that the precipitate is enriched in Nb, Mo, W and C while depleted in Fe, Mn and Al. Subfigure (i) is the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern indexed to the [110] zone axis, confirming the precipitate has a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure with a lattice parameter of 4.36 Å, identified as (Nb,Mo,W)C carbide. These results verify the composition and crystal structure of the submicron precipitates, supporting analysis of precipitation behavior and strengthening mechanisms in the hot-rolled low-density steel.

This set of figures presents TEM characterization results of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel with different hot rolling reductions. Subfigures (a-c) are selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns along [100] zone axis taken from austenite matrix of HR45 (45% reduction, a&d), HR68 (68% reduction, b&e) and HR82 (82% reduction, c&f) samples, with a scale bar of 5 1/nm. The faint superlattice spots circled in SAED patterns confirm the presence of L'12-type κ-carbides. Subfigures (d-f) are corresponding dark field (DF) TEM images captured using (100) superlattice diffraction, with 30 nm scale bar, revealing homogeneously distributed nano-sized κ-carbides inside austenite grains. The κ-carbides have average sizes of 0.81±0.26 nm, 0.78±0.17 nm, 0.83±0.23 nm and volume fractions of 3.5±0.33%, 3.3±0.52%, 3.9±0.39% for HR45, HR68 and HR82 respectively, showing no significant difference with increasing hot rolling reduction. These coherent nano-precipitates provide ~60 MPa precipitation strengthening, contributing to the enhanced mechanical properties of the steels.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples fabricated with varied hot-rolling reductions. Subplot (a) shows room-temperature engineering stress-strain curves for three samples: HR45 (45% hot-rolling reduction), HR68 (68% reduction), and HR82 (82% reduction). With the increase of hot-rolling reduction, both yield strength and ultimate tensile strength increase notably (ultimate tensile strength rises from ~861 MPa for HR45 to ~976 MPa for HR82, yield strength rises from 480 MPa to 648 MPa), while total elongation slightly decreases from 60% to 50%, all samples retain favorable ductility. Subplot (b) exhibits Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy plotted against hot-rolling reduction. The impact energy decreases linearly from 133 ± 2.5 J/m² at 45% reduction to 105 ± 1.2 J/m² at 82% reduction, with all samples maintaining impact energy above 100 J/m², indicating excellent toughness retention. This figure supports the core conclusion that increasing hot-rolling reduction effectively enhances the strength of the investigated steel while preserving acceptable ductility and toughness, achieving an excellent strength-plasticity-toughness combination.

This set of SEM fractography images presents tensile fracture surfaces of three Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples with different hot rolling reductions: (a) HR45 (45% rolling reduction), (b) HR68 (68% rolling reduction), (c) HR82 (82% rolling reduction), with a 20 μm scale bar applied to all subgraphs. Ductile dimples are marked by white circles, while microvoids are indicated by white arrows in each subgraph. All three samples show typical ductile fracture characteristics, consisting of abundant dimples and dispersed microvoids, where microvoids preferentially nucleate at (Nb,Mo,W)C particle-austenite matrix interfaces to relieve strain/stress concentration. As hot rolling reduction increases, the average size of dimples decreases gradually, which matches the grain refinement trend with higher rolling reduction. All samples retain ductile fracture morphology, verifying that the toughness of the investigated steel is barely impaired by increased rolling reduction, which is consistent with Charpy impact test results, and proves that intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C precipitates exert no negative effect on the steel's toughness.

This plot presents the relationship between mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels, with the x-axis referring to yield strength Rₚ₀.₂ (MPa), left y-axis referring to Charpy impact energy (J/cm²), and right y-axis referring to total elongation (%). Multiple marker types represent property data of various steel grades from references, while green star markers correspond to the three experimental steels in this work subjected to 45%, 68%, and 82% hot rolling reduction respectively. A general decreasing trend of both Charpy impact energy and total elongation with rising yield strength is observed for all plotted materials. The experimental steels in this work, with yield strength ranging from ~480 MPa to ~648 MPa, exhibit far higher impact energy (>100 J/cm²) and total elongation (50-60%) than other steels at equivalent strength levels, achieving an excellent strength-ductility-toughness balance. This figure validates that increasing hot rolling reduction is an effective approach to optimize the comprehensive mechanical properties of this type of low-density steel.

This figure consists of two subplots showing microstructural parameter variations of Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W austenitic low-density steel under different hot-rolling reductions (45%, 68%, 82%, corresponding to samples HR45, HR68, HR82). Subplot (a) quantifies mean grain size excluding twins, effective grain size including twins, and dislocation density: as hot-rolling reduction increases, both grain sizes decrease (grain size without twins from ~45 μm to ~19 μm, effective grain size with twins from ~22 μm to ~7 μm), while dislocation density rises from ~2.7×10^13 m^-2 to ~9.9×10^13 m^-2. Subplot (b) shows volume fractions and mean sizes of (Nb,Mo,W)C and κ-carbide precipitates: (Nb,Mo,W)C has stable volume fraction ~0.2% and mean size ~260 nm across all samples; κ-carbide maintains mean size ~0.8 nm, with volume fraction slightly fluctuating between 3.3% and 3.9%. The figure supports that grain boundary strengthening and dislocation strengthening are the main contributors to strength improvement with higher hot-rolling reduction, while precipitation strengthening contribution remains nearly unchanged.

This figure presents strengthening mechanism analysis for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples processed under different hot rolling reduction ratios (HR45: 45%, HR68: 68%, HR82: 82%). Subplot (a) shows the relationship between σ_F0 (true flow stress at yielding, calculated by subtracting precipitate strengthening σ_p and dislocation strengthening σ_ds from total yield strength σ_y) and the inverse square root of effective grain size d_e^-1/2. The red line is Hall-Petch fitting, giving the equation σ_F0 = 203.1 + 733.4 d_e^-1/2, with friction stress 203.1 MPa and Hall-Petch coefficient 733.4 MPa·μm^1/2, matching the three measured points for effective grain sizes 21.82 μm (359.5 MPa), 11.55 μm (420.0 MPa), 7.02 μm (479.3 MPa). Subplot (b) is a stacked bar chart showing contributions of individual strengthening mechanisms (lattice friction stress σ_F, precipitate shearing strengthening σ_p-shearing, Orowan precipitate strengthening σ_p-Orowan, dislocation strengthening σ_ds, grain boundary strengthening σ_gb) to yield strength, with triangles marking experimentally measured yield strength. Calculated yield strength values match experimental values well. Grain boundary strengthening is the dominant strengthening mechanism, and the increase of grain boundary and dislocation strengthening contributions with higher hot rolling reduction accounts for the improved yield strength of the steel.

This figure presents true stress (σ_t, solid lines, unit: MPa) - true strain (ε_t) curves and strain hardening rate (dσ/dε, dotted lines, unit: MPa) - true strain curves for three Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples processed with different hot rolling reductions: HR45 (45% reduction), HR68 (68% reduction), HR82 (82% reduction). All samples exhibit a three-stage strain hardening behavior: an initial rapid drop after yielding (Stage I), a gradual rise to peak value (Stage II), and a continuous decrease until fracture (Stage III). With increasing hot rolling reduction, both the true stress level and strain hardening rate increase, with HR82 showing the highest strength. Arrows mark the true strain values (ε_e = 0.1, 0.2, 0.4) where tensile tests were interrupted. The three-stage hardening behavior maintains a high strain hardening rate of ~2000 MPa over a wide plastic strain range, contributing to excellent ductility of the steel, and the results support that higher hot rolling reduction can effectively improve the mechanical properties of this low-density steel.

This group of TEM bright-field images, captured along the <110> zone axis of the austenite matrix in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples, reveal deformation substructures after different engineering strains. Subfigures (a,d,g) correspond to samples HR45 (45% hot rolling reduction), HR68 (68% reduction), HR82 (82% reduction) at 0.1 engineering strain respectively, showing {111} slip plane traces marked by dashed lines, with slip band spacing decreasing from ~296 nm for HR45 to ~68 nm for HR82. Subfigures (b,e,h) show samples at 0.2 engineering strain, with denser slip bands, and ill-defined microbands (marked by arrows) have already formed in HR68 and HR82. Subfigures (c,f,i) show samples at 0.4 engineering strain, with distinct-boundary microbands marked by solid lines. The results demonstrate that deformation substructures evolve from planar slip bands, Taylor lattice to microbands with increasing strain; higher hot rolling reduction, which leads to finer initial grain size, accelerates deformation substructure evolution, causing earlier saturation of slip band refinement and earlier microband formation, which explains the difference in three-stage strain hardening behavior among the three samples.

This line chart presents the evolution of slip band spacing (unit: nm) as a function of engineering strain for three Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel samples subjected to different hot rolling reductions: HR45 (45% reduction, gray square marker), HR68 (68% reduction, red circle marker), and HR82 (82% reduction, blue triangle marker). For all samples, slip band spacing decreases with increasing engineering strain initially. At an engineering strain of 0.1, the slip band spacing of HR45, HR68, HR82 is 296.40 ± 50.06 nm, 155.02 ± 38.42 nm, 68.05 ± 8.91 nm respectively, showing that higher rolling reduction (and finer initial grain size) leads to smaller initial slip band spacing. All samples reach a saturation slip band spacing of ~50 nm when engineering strain reaches ~0.2, with no further reduction at higher strain. These results confirm that finer grain size accelerates slip band refinement, and the saturation of slip band refinement at ~50 nm explains the strain hardening behavior differences among samples, supporting the dynamic slip band refinement model.
